expanded_description: Comprehensive review of all stoichiometry concepts including mole ratios, mass calculations, particle conversions, and problem-solving strategies.

core_concept.principle: Stoichiometry connects all quantitative aspects of chemical reactions through a unified framework centered on the mole concept and balanced equations.
core_concept.explanation: This review integrates all stoichiometry skills: balancing equations, extracting mole ratios, converting between mass-moles-particles, and solving multi-step problems. Mastery requires understanding how all these concepts work together in a systematic problem-solving approach.

image.alt: Diagram showing all stoichiometry concepts connected
image.caption: All stoichiometry concepts connect through the mole and balanced equations

sections.introduction: This section reviews all stoichiometry concepts in an integrated framework. Use this as a reference when solving any stoichiometry problem.

key_concept.definition: The complete stoichiometry framework: Balanced Equation → Mole Ratios → Conversion Pathway (mass/moles/particles) → Calculate → Verify
key_concept.context: Every stoichiometry problem follows this framework regardless of the specific quantities involved.

principles.statement: All stoichiometric conversions between different substances must pass through moles using mole ratios from balanced equations.
principles.application: Never attempt direct mass-to-mass or particle-to-particle conversions between different substances without using moles.
principles.example: To convert g A to g B: $\text{g A} \rightarrow \text{mol A} \rightarrow \text{mol B} \rightarrow \text{g B}$
principles.analogy: Like exchanging currencies through a common base currency—moles are the universal currency of chemistry.

principles.statement: Mass and atoms are conserved in chemical reactions; balanced equations reflect this conservation.
principles.application: Always verify equations are balanced before calculations; use conservation to check answers.
principles.example: Total reactant mass = Total product mass (in closed systems)
principles.analogy: Like a financial budget—money in must equal money out; atoms in must equal atoms out.

principles.statement: Coefficients in balanced equations represent mole ratios, not mass ratios.
principles.application: Use coefficients only in mole-to-mole conversions, not in molar mass calculations.
principles.example: In $2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$: coefficients give 2:1:2 mole ratio, not mass ratio
principles.analogy: Like recipe proportions—'2 cups flour' tells you the ratio, not the weight.

components.definition: Stoichiometry requires three types of conversion factors: molar mass (g/mol), Avogadro's constant ($6.022 \times 10^{23}$ particles/mol), and mole ratios (mol/mol).
components.interpretation: Each arrow represents one or more conversion steps. The middle step (mole ratio) is required only when changing substances.

image.alt: Flowchart showing all stoichiometry conversion pathways
image.caption: All stoichiometry conversions follow this pathway through moles

worked_examples.title: Review Problem 1: Complete Mass-to-Mass Calculation
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the mass of $\text{NH}_3$ produced from $25.0$ g of $\text{N}_2$. ($\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NH}_3$)

step_by_step.explanation: Check atom counts: 2 N and 6 H on both sides
Use mole ratio from coefficients: $\dfrac{2 \text{ mol } \text{NH}_3}{1 \text{ mol } \text{N}_2}$

worked_examples.concept_applied: Complete mass-to-mass pathway: g → mol → mol → g

worked_examples.title: Review Problem 2: Mass to Particle Conversion
worked_examples.problem: How many molecules of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ are produced from $10.0$ g of $\text{H}_2$? ($2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Mass to particle pathway: g → mol → mol → particles

worked_examples.title: Review Problem 3: Particle to Mass Conversion
worked_examples.problem: What mass of $\text{O}_2$ is needed to react with $3.01 \times 10^{23}$ molecules of $\text{H}_2$? ($2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Particle to mass pathway: particles → mol → mol → g

sections.key_concept: Different stoichiometry problems require different conversion pathways. This summary helps you identify which pathway to use.

classifications.value: Simplest stoichiometry type; uses only mole ratio from balanced equation
classifications.behavior: Multiply given moles by appropriate mole ratio

examples.process: Given mol A, find mol B
examples.explanation: $\text{mol B} = \text{mol A} \times \dfrac{\text{coeff B}}{\text{coeff A}}$
$5.0$ mol $\text{N}_2 \times \dfrac{2 \text{ mol } \text{NH}_3}{1 \text{ mol } \text{N}_2} = 10.0$ mol $\text{NH}_3$

classifications.value: Most common stoichiometry type; uses molar masses and mole ratio

examples.process: Given g A, find g B
examples.explanation: $\text{g B} = \text{g A} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol A}}{M_A} \times \dfrac{\text{coeff B}}{\text{coeff A}} \times \dfrac{M_B}{1 \text{ mol B}}$
$10.0$ g $\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 89.4$ g $\text{H}_2\text{O}$

classifications.value: Uses Avogadro's constant in addition to molar mass and mole ratio
classifications.behavior: Pathway always passes through moles: particles ↔ mol ↔ mol ↔ particles/mass

examples.process: Given particles A, find particles B
examples.explanation: particles A → mol A → mol B → particles B

examples.process: Given mass A, find particles B
examples.explanation: g A → mol A → mol B → particles B

classifications.value: Combines multiple conversion types in complex problems

examples.process: Given g A, find atoms of element in B
examples.explanation: g A → mol A → mol B → mol atoms → atoms (5 steps)
Find H atoms in water from grams of methane

worked_examples.title: Review Problem 4: Identifying Conversion Type
worked_examples.problem: Identify the conversion type and pathway for: 'How many grams of $\text{CO}_2$ form from $5.0$ mol of $\text{O}_2$?'

step_by_step.calculation: Type: mol → mol → g (2 conversion steps)

worked_examples.final_answer: Conversion type: mole-to-mass; Pathway: mol $\text{O}_2$ → mol $\text{CO}_2$ → g $\text{CO}_2$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Identifying conversion type determines the pathway.

worked_examples.title: Review Problem 5: Complex Mixed Conversion
worked_examples.problem: How many oxygen atoms are in the $\text{CO}_2$ produced from $20.0$ g of $\text{CH}_4$? ($\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$)

step_by_step.explanation: g $\text{CH}_4$ → mol $\text{CH}_4$ → mol $\text{CO}_2$ → mol O atoms → O atoms

step_by_step.calculation: $? = 20.0 \text{ g} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol } \text{CH}_4}{16.04 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol } \text{CO}_2}{1 \text{ mol } \text{CH}_4} \times \dfrac{2 \text{ mol O}}{1 \text{ mol } \text{CO}_2} \times \dfrac{6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ atoms}}{1 \text{ mol O}}$

step_by_step.explanation: Multiply across numerator, divide by denominator

worked_examples.concept_applied: Complex conversions chain multiple factors through moles.

sections.introduction: A systematic approach prevents errors in all stoichiometry problems. Use this checklist for every calculation.

key_concept.definition: The 7-step stoichiometry checklist: (1) Balance equation, (2) Identify given/unknown, (3) Map pathway, (4) Gather factors, (5) Set up dimensional analysis, (6) Calculate, (7) Verify.
key_concept.context: Following this checklist consistently prevents common errors and ensures accurate results.

process_flow.description: Never skip this step—unbalanced equations give wrong mole ratios
process_flow.mechanism: Count atoms of each element on both sides; adjust coefficients

process_flow.description: Write down exactly what you have and what you need with units
process_flow.mechanism: Label substance and quantity type (g, mol, particles)

process_flow.description: Plan the route from given to unknown through moles
process_flow.mechanism: Draw pathway: given → mol → mol → unknown

process_flow.description: Collect all needed factors before calculating
process_flow.mechanism: Molar masses, mole ratios, Avogadro's constant, subscript ratios

process_flow.description: Chain all factors with proper unit cancellation
process_flow.mechanism: Arrange so unwanted units cancel, desired unit remains

process_flow.description: Keep extra digits during calculation; round only final answer
process_flow.mechanism: Use calculator memory or write full values

process_flow.description: Check units, significant figures, reasonableness, and mass balance

worked_examples.title: Review Problem 6: Applying the Complete Strategy
worked_examples.problem: Use the 7-step checklist to solve: What mass of $\text{AlCl}_3$ forms from $15.0$ g of $\text{Cl}_2$? ($2\text{Al} + 3\text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{AlCl}_3$)

step_by_step.explanation: 2 Al and 6 Cl on both sides
Chain all factors with unit cancellation
step_by_step.calculation: $? = 15.0 \text{ g} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol}}{70.90 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{2 \text{ mol } \text{AlCl}_3}{3 \text{ mol } \text{Cl}_2} \times \dfrac{133.33 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ mol}}$

step_by_step.explanation: Multiply and divide without intermediate rounding
Units: g ✓; Sig figs: 3 ✓; Reasonable: similar molar masses, 2:3 ratio ✓

worked_examples.concept_applied: The 7-step checklist ensures accurate, verified results.

sections.key_concept: Understanding common mistakes helps you avoid them. Review these errors before taking assessments.

classifications.behavior: Always verify atom counts on both sides before calculating

examples.explanation: Gives 1:1 ratio instead of correct 2:1 $\text{H}_2$:$\text{O}_2$
Gives correct 2:1 ratio
Oxygen gas is $\text{O}_2$ ($32.00$ g/mol), not O ($16.00$ g/mol)
Must count 2 H atoms: $18.02$ g/mol, not $17.01$ g/mol

classifications.value: Using wrong mole ratio or wrong orientation
classifications.behavior: Place given substance in denominator, unknown in numerator

examples.process: Wrong: $\dfrac{1 \text{ mol } \text{N}_2}{2 \text{ mol } \text{NH}_3}$ when converting $\text{N}_2$ to $\text{NH}_3$
examples.explanation: Should use $\dfrac{2 \text{ mol } \text{NH}_3}{1 \text{ mol } \text{N}_2}$

classifications.value: Skipping the mole step or using wrong sequence
classifications.behavior: Always pass through moles when changing substances

examples.process: Wrong: g A → g B directly
examples.explanation: Must use: g A → mol A → mol B → g B

classifications.value: Rounding too early or arithmetic mistakes
classifications.behavior: Keep extra digits during calculation; round only final answer

examples.process: Rounding moles to 2 sig figs mid-calculation
examples.explanation: Should keep 4+ digits, round final answer only

classifications.behavior: Always verify using checklist before submitting

examples.explanation: Check units, sig figs, reasonableness, mass balance

worked_examples.title: Review Problem 7: Error Identification
worked_examples.problem: A student calculated that $20.0$ g $\text{CH}_4$ produces $20.0$ g $\text{CO}_2$. Identify all errors. ($\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$)

step_by_step.explanation: Student assumed reactant mass = product mass for individual substances
step_by_step.calculation: Wrong: only TOTAL mass is conserved
Must use: g → mol → mol → g pathway

$20.0 \text{ g} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol}}{16.04 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol } \text{CO}_2}{1 \text{ mol } \text{CH}_4} \times \dfrac{44.01 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ mol}} = 54.9 \text{ g } \text{CO}_2$

worked_examples.final_answer: Correct: $54.9$ g $\text{CO}_2$. Errors: assumed individual mass conservation, skipped moles, no verification
worked_examples.concept_applied: Multiple errors compound; systematic approach prevents all.

sections.introduction: Test your stoichiometry mastery with these comprehensive problems. Try solving before looking at solutions.

key_concept.definition: Self-assessment problems cover all stoichiometry types. Use these to identify areas needing more practice.
key_concept.context: Cover solutions and attempt each problem independently, then check your work.

worked_examples.title: Self-Assessment Problem 1
worked_examples.problem: How many moles of $\text{O}_2$ are needed to react with $6.0$ moles of $\text{H}_2$? ($2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$)

worked_examples.title: Self-Assessment Problem 2
worked_examples.problem: What mass of $\text{NaCl}$ forms from $10.0$ g of Na? ($2\text{Na} + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl}$)

step_by_step.explanation: g Na → mol Na → mol NaCl → g NaCl
step_by_step.calculation: $? = 10.0 \text{ g} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol}}{22.99 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{2 \text{ mol NaCl}}{2 \text{ mol Na}} \times \dfrac{58.44 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ mol}}$

worked_examples.title: Self-Assessment Problem 3
worked_examples.problem: How many molecules of $\text{NH}_3$ are produced from $5.00$ g of $\text{N}_2$? ($\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NH}_3$)

step_by_step.calculation: $? = 5.00 \text{ g} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol}}{28.02 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{2 \text{ mol } \text{NH}_3}{1 \text{ mol } \text{N}_2} \times \dfrac{6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ molecules}}{1 \text{ mol}}$

step_by_step.explanation: Multiply and divide, round to 3 significant figures

worked_examples.title: Self-Assessment Problem 4
worked_examples.problem: Verify conservation of mass: $10.0$ g $\text{H}_2$ + $79.4$ g $\text{O}_2$ → $89.4$ g $\text{H}_2\text{O}$

step_by_step.explanation: Product mass should equal total reactant mass
Check that reactant mass equals product mass

worked_examples.final_answer: Mass is conserved: $89.4$ g reactants = $89.4$ g products
worked_examples.concept_applied: Law of Conservation of Mass verification.

worked_examples.title: Self-Assessment Problem 5
worked_examples.problem: What mass of $\text{O}_2$ is needed to burn $25.0$ g of $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$? ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 3\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$)

step_by_step.calculation: $? = 25.0 \text{ g} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol}}{44.09 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{5 \text{ mol } \text{O}_2}{1 \text{ mol } \text{C}_3\text{H}_8} \times \dfrac{32.00 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ mol}}$

step_by_step.explanation: Multiply and divide, round to 3 significant figures

sections.key_concept: Keep this reference guide handy for all stoichiometry problems.

categories.description: These conversion factors are used in all stoichiometry calculations.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Memorize these relationships for efficient problem-solving.

examples.explanation: g ↔ mol: $\text{mol} = \dfrac{\text{g}}{M}$; $\text{g} = \text{mol} \times M$
mol ↔ particles: $\text{particles} = \text{mol} \times N_A$; $\text{mol} = \dfrac{\text{particles}}{N_A}$
mol A ↔ mol B: $\text{mol B} = \text{mol A} \times \dfrac{\text{coeff B}}{\text{coeff A}}$

categories.description: These seven elements exist as diatomic molecules in their elemental form.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Always use molecular molar mass (not atomic) for these elements.

examples.process: H, N, O, F, Cl, Br, I
examples.explanation: Remember: HONClBrI or 'Have No Fear Of Ice Cold Beer'

categories.description: Apply these rules to all stoichiometry calculations.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Final answer should match least precise given value.

examples.explanation: Answer has same sig figs as value with fewest sig figs
Answer has same decimal places as value with fewest decimal places
Keep extra digits during calculation; round only final answer

categories.description: Use this checklist before finalizing any stoichiometry answer.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Systematic verification catches errors before they become final answers.

examples.explanation: Count atoms of each element on both sides
Verify only desired unit remains
Match least precise given value
Estimate expected magnitude; compare to calculated
Total reactant mass = Total product mass

summary: Stoichiometry is the quantitative study of relationships between reactants and products in chemical reactions. All stoichiometric calculations follow a unified framework: (1) Write and balance the chemical equation, (2) Identify given and unknown quantities, (3) Map the conversion pathway through moles, (4) Gather conversion factors (molar mass, mole ratio, Avogadro's constant), (5) Set up dimensional analysis with proper unit cancellation, (6) Calculate without intermediate rounding, (7) Verify the answer. Key principles: moles are the central hub for all conversions between different substances; coefficients provide mole ratios, not mass ratios; mass and atoms are conserved in reactions. Three main conversion types: mole-to-mole (simplest, one step), mass-to-mass (most common, three steps), particle conversions (uses Avogadro's constant). Common errors include using unbalanced equations, incorrect molar masses (especially for diatomic elements), inverted mole ratios, skipping the mole step, rounding too early, and failing to verify. Seven diatomic elements (H, N, O, F, Cl, Br, I) require molecular molar masses. Significant figures should match the least precise given value. A systematic 7-step checklist and verification process ensures accurate, reliable results. Mastery of stoichiometry is essential for all quantitative chemistry including limiting reactant problems, percent yield calculations, solution stoichiometry, and advanced chemical analysis. Regular practice with varied problem types builds confidence and prevents common mistakes.
